paragraphs.text: Skyscanner welcomes the opportunity to respond to the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response

paragraphs.text: The European Union (EU) has often pioneered consumer protections in both the offline and online economy, with its legislation serving as a template for other jurisdictions. As a result, European consumers enjoy a very high level of protection from unfair commercial practices.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response

paragraphs.text: This is to be commended. Consumers must be able to make well-informed decisions about the goods and services they purchase, for example. This not only has clear benefits for consumers themselves – such as ensuring that they do not spend more money than they need to – but also for businesses and the economy more generally.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response

evidence_quotes.quote: unscrupulous actors cannot unfairly benefit by undercutting compliant businesses

paragraphs.text: High standards that are rigorously enforced ensure that unscrupulous actors cannot unfairly benefit by undercutting compliant businesses, while consumers are more likely to purchase innovative new goods or services if they are confident that they have been able to make a well-informed decision and that they will be protected if things go wrong.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response

paragraphs.text: As a travel metasearch service, Skyscanner understands the importance of consumer trust. Booking travel can often be an overwhelming and stressful experience. These purchases will often be among the most expensive that a consumer will make during a given year, while many trips will also be of significant personal or sentimental value (such as a family holiday or a trip home to see loved ones). Consumers often have to choose between multiple competing providers and digest a substantial amount of information. Skyscanner exists to make this process as easy as possible. Yet we can only do this if people trust that the information on our site, including prices, is accurate and reliable. In this way, high consumer protection standards in the EU have helped our business model by boosting standards across the wider industry.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response

paragraphs.text: As more and more goods and services are bought and sold online, it is right that the European Commission actively monitors the extent to which existing consumer protection laws are fit for purpose in the digital age. We agree with the Commission’s Fitness Check on digital fairness that there are some issues, including fragmented enforcement between Member States and sometimes unclear or overlapping obligations.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response

paragraphs.text: This legal uncertainty increases compliance costs for businesses operating across the EU, and a concerted effort to align enforcement and clarify how obligations apply in certain contexts could ease compliance burdens and align with the competitiveness drive.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response

paragraphs.text: However, we do not believe that there is clear evidence of broad legal gaps in the current consumer protection architecture of the EU when applied to online practices

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response

paragraphs.text: more generally. This is particularly the case when considering that in recent years a whole host of additional digital laws with consumer provisions – including the Digital Services Act (DSA), Digital Markets Act (DMA), and AI Act – have only just been implemented or, in the case of the AI Act, are still being implemented.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response

evidence_quotes.quote: Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD)
Consumer Rights Directive (CRD)
General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR)
ePrivacy Directive
Platform to Business Regulation (P2B)
Air Services Regulation

paragraphs.text: These new digital regulations, which were not fully considered by the 2024 Fitness Check, complement numerous further foundational regulations including the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD), Consumer Rights Directive (CRD), the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and ePrivacy Directive, Platform to Business Regulation (P2B), as well as sectoral regulations including the Air Services Regulation. These laws are technologically neutral and apply equally to offline and online practices.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response

paragraphs.text: In a context where this existing corpus of consumer law is already creating legal uncertainty for businesses and hesitant enforcement by national authorities due to overlapping requirements, we believe that introducing additional legal requirements would only exacerbate this problem.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response

evidence_quotes.quote: harmonising enforcement between member states

paragraphs.text: We believe that it would make much more sense to focus efforts on harmonising enforcement between member states, by producing clear guidance on how existing rules apply in specific online contexts and how different legal texts interact with each other.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response

paragraphs.text: Rather than adding new rules on top of existing ones that are not being properly enforced, a renewed focus on addressing implementation challenges with current laws would better balance the need to maintain high consumer protection standards with the EU's competitiveness drive.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response

evidence_quotes.quote: We do not believe that new rules on dark patterns are needed at this stage.
User interface designs that deliberately mislead consumers, or lead them to make a decision that they otherwise wouldn't have, already breach EU consumer law.

paragraphs.text: We do not believe that new rules on dark patterns are needed at this stage. They are already regulated by different pieces of EU legislation, from multiple angles. User interface designs that deliberately mislead consumers, or lead them to make a decision that they otherwise wouldn't have, already breach EU consumer law. For example:

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response

paragraphs.text: The UCPD already prohibits unfair commercial practices that distort consumer behaviour, with Annex I listing specific practices that are banned.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response

evidence_quotes.quote: consumers be clearly informed when entering into a binding contract that requires payment.

paragraphs.text: The CRD prevents material information from being obscured or ambiguous (Articles 6 and 8), with Article 8(2) requiring that consumers be clearly informed when entering into a binding contract that requires payment.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response

evidence_quotes.quote: automatic pre-selection of add-ons.

paragraphs.text: There are also requirements around express consent for any additional costs (Article 22), which prevents the automatic pre-selection of add-ons.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response

evidence_quotes.quote: ambiguous language in the presentation of choices, presenting choices in a leading manner (such as automatically selecting or highlighting a

paragraphs.text: We believe that these requirements already ban many practices listed in this consultation, including ambiguous language in the presentation of choices, presenting choices in a leading manner (such as automatically selecting or highlighting a

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns
subscription-based price with insufficient information on the terms and conditions)

paragraphs.text: subscription-based price with insufficient information on the terms and conditions), nagging, or sneaking into the online basket. Indeed, the Commission's Notice on the interpretation and application of the UCPD confirm that the Directive can be used to challenge the fairness of dark patterns when employed by business-to-consumer commercial relationships (section 4.2.7).

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns

paragraphs.text: These rules are complemented by multiple requirements under the GDPR and EDPB guidelines on dark patterns that outline how consent must be freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous, with consumers given clear, accessible information on how their data will be used.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response

evidence_quotes.quote: There are also requirements on dark patterns and deceptive techniques under the DMA and AI Act.
There are further obligations around the transparency of recommender systems and user controls.

paragraphs.text: Meanwhile, the DSA also imposes obligations on platforms to design their user interfaces in such a way that allows users to make free, informed decisions. Deceptive and distortive practices are incompatible with this requirement. There are further obligations around the transparency of recommender systems and user controls. There are also requirements on dark patterns and deceptive techniques under the DMA and AI Act.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns identified in the consultation
presenting choices in a leading manner

paragraphs.text: In short, we believe that the problematic dark patterns identified in the consultation can already be addressed under existing law. Adding new, broad prohibitions on specific practices could risk businesses deciding to remove features that are genuinely helpful to consumers (such as badges or logos used to identify businesses with the best user experience, as used on Skyscanner). User interface designs that differentiate between different suppliers can be vital to help consumers make informed choices more quickly, but could fall foul of a broadly scoped ban on "presenting choices in a leading manner", for example.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response

evidence_quotes.quote: Effective personalisation is of significant benefit to consumers.

paragraphs.text: Effective personalisation is of significant benefit to consumers. In travel, it allows consumers to find more relevant content more quickly, reducing search costs substantially. Personalised pricing can also allow businesses to offer consumers discounts (for example, to consumers who are part of a loyalty programme), while personalised advertising ensures that consumers see more relevant adverts, improving their online experience.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response

evidence_quotes.quote: Personalisation is also a key driver of greater business efficiency and competitiveness.
Personalised pricing, meanwhile, is an important means for businesses to build customer loyalty and to reward consumers for their continued use of their service.

paragraphs.text: Personalisation is also a key driver of greater business efficiency and competitiveness. When it comes to advertising, personalisation can allow businesses to make their marketing more efficient, by running more targeted campaigns with higher engagement and thus return on investment. This frees up money for businesses to invest in new product features, or to hire more staff, for example. Personalised pricing, meanwhile, is an important means for businesses to build customer loyalty and to reward consumers for their continued use of their service. In this way, it drives greater competition between businesses.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response

evidence_quotes.quote: Personalisation can also help businesses to deliver wider social goods.

paragraphs.text: Personalisation can also help businesses to deliver wider social goods. For example, it can help online businesses to deliver more accessible experiences for their users with

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response

evidence_quotes.quote: surface alternative, less busy destinations that are similar to ones previously visited by a user

paragraphs.text: particular needs, as well as allowing businesses to surface alternative, less busy destinations that are similar to ones previously visited by a user.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response

evidence_quotes.quote: unfair use of personalisation

paragraphs.text: Given all this, it is extremely important that the Commission distinguish between the unfair use of personalisation and its legitimate counterpart, given the significant benefits it provides to consumers and businesses when used fairly. Clearly, the use of personal information to raise prices for certain types of users only is not the same as offering discounts to members of a loyalty programme.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns
regulate personalisation

paragraphs.text: As with dark patterns, there are already many obligations that regulate personalisation in both pricing and advertising. For example:

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response

evidence_quotes.quote: prices are personalised based on profiling.

paragraphs.text: The UCPD prohibits misleading or unduly influential personalised commercial practices, while the Omnibus Directive mandates disclosure when prices are personalised based on profiling.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response

evidence_quotes.quote: The GDPR restricts profiling absent a lawful, transparent and fair basis

paragraphs.text: The GDPR restricts profiling absent a lawful, transparent and fair basis, as well as granting individuals rights related to automated decision-making.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response

evidence_quotes.quote: a recommender system that is not based on profiling.
prohibits the use of profiling for ads delivered to minors.

paragraphs.text: The DSA imposes transparency obligations on the functioning of recommendation systems, and gives users the option of a recommender system that is not based on profiling. It also prohibits the use of profiling for ads delivered to minors.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response

evidence_quotes.quote: personalisation practices.

paragraphs.text: Meanwhile, both the DMA and the AI Act also impose obligations on in-scope services with regards to personalisation practices.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response

evidence_quotes.quote: advertising by social media influencers.

paragraphs.text: We believe that the law is already clear regarding advertising by social media influencers. In particular, several laws already require influencers to disclose advertising clearly and prominently. The UCPD, under point 11 of its Annex, prohibits undisclosed advertorials, which the Commission has clarified extends to influencers. This practice is always considered unfair, given its potential to mislead consumers, and it covers endorsements, barters and affiliate marketing.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response
Harmful practices by social media influencers

evidence_quotes.quote: Any trader, including the one benefitting from the advertising, can be liable for failure to comply with this part of the UCPD.

paragraphs.text: Any trader, including the one benefitting from the advertising, can be liable for failure to comply with this part of the UCPD.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response
Harmful practices by social media influencers

evidence_quotes.quote: undisclosed advertorials

paragraphs.text: Given this, and how clear the existing law and guidance has been, we do not believe that further rules are required. Requiring brands and agencies to take additional measures to ensure that influencers comply with the law (and potentially all applicable laws beyond the rules on undisclosed advertorials) would risk becoming extremely burdensome.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response
Harmful practices by social media influencers

paragraphs.text: This could make it harder for businesses operating in the EU to use a marketing channel that is growing in importance and relevance to both businesses and consumers.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act – Skyscanner’s response
Harmful practices by social media influencers

evidence_quotes.quote: pricing information that does not correspond with the actual price

paragraphs.text: The transparency and accuracy of the pricing information on our site is the main way that we build consumer trust. If our users are presented with pricing information that does not correspond with the actual price (for example if the price were to change when a user re-directs to one of our partner's websites), they would not return to Skyscanner.

evidence_quotes.quote: pricing is clear and accurate.

paragraphs.text: We therefore take this issue very seriously and are supportive of rules to ensure that pricing is clear and accurate. As with the other issues outlined in this consultation, there is already a large number of rules that require price transparency. These rules should, in the first instance, be clarified and enforced more uniformly before the Commission adds new rules.

evidence_quotes.quote: For example, drip pricing can already clearly fall foul of the UCPD when it is misleading or deceptive, such as price increases that occur late in the booking process or advertising a lower price than what is actually offered.

paragraphs.text: For example, drip pricing can already clearly fall foul of the UCPD when it is misleading or deceptive, such as price increases that occur late in the booking process or advertising a lower price than what is actually offered. Existing law already requires traders to present prices to consumers inclusive of all mandatory costs, or to inform them that mandatory costs may be payable when these cannot be calculated in advance. Meanwhile, the Commission has carried out numerous enforcement sweeps that have led to substantial action against unfair pricing practices when found to infringe EU law.

evidence_quotes.quote: drip pricing

paragraphs.text: It is not clear what a more explicit ban on drip pricing could achieve that greater enforcement of existing law (which we believe is already clear enough) could not.

evidence_quotes.quote: advertising of 'starting' prices and price comparisons

paragraphs.text: We also believe that the Commission's proposals on the advertising of 'starting' prices and price comparisons could have unintended consequences that will make it harder for consumers to make well-informed, quick purchasing decisions.

evidence_quotes.quote: prices can not only fluctuate in response to supply and demand but also where there are multiple operators or suppliers to choose from.

paragraphs.text: First, the use of 'starting' prices is, in our view, well understood by consumers. In travel it is an important means of giving consumers an initial indication of prices in a sector where prices can not only fluctuate in response to supply and demand but also where there are multiple operators or suppliers to choose from.

evidence_quotes.quote: The misleading or deceptive use of 'starting' prices

paragraphs.text: The misleading or deceptive use of 'starting' prices is already substantially regulated in the EU, under both the UCPD and the Price Indication Directive (PID).

evidence_quotes.quote: "attractive 'starting' prices whilst automatically applying dynamic price increases (rendering such starting prices unrealistic for a majority of buyers)"

paragraphs.text: Specifically, the advertising of "attractive 'starting' prices whilst automatically applying dynamic price increases (rendering such starting prices unrealistic for a majority of buyers)" is clearly an unfair practice and is already against the law. We do not believe that there should be further limits placed on legitimate and non-deceptive 'starting' prices when any dynamic pricing is also used, given the risk of unintended consequences.

evidence_quotes.quote: dynamic pricing is used widely in the travel sector by operators to respond to

paragraphs.text: While Skyscanner, as an intermediary, does not set the base fare offered by airlines or hotels, dynamic pricing is used widely in the travel sector by operators to respond to

evidence_quotes.quote: dynamic pricing

paragraphs.text: changes in supply and demand. It is an entirely legitimate and long-established practice that allows consumers to benefit from cheaper prices when demand is softer and enables businesses to fill their inventory more efficiently. This can also have broader social benefits – for example, dynamic pricing can allow airlines to ensure that their aircraft are as full as possible, reducing the environmental impact of flying on a per passenger basis.

evidence_quotes.quote: Dynamic pricing in response to changes in supply and demand, which also often relies on AI algorithms for greater efficacy, is not the same as surveillance pricing, which uses sensitive personal data to raise the price for a particular good or service only for a specific consumer (or type of consumer).

paragraphs.text: Dynamic pricing in response to changes in supply and demand, which also often relies on AI algorithms for greater efficacy, is not the same as surveillance pricing, which uses sensitive personal data to raise the price for a particular good or service only for a specific consumer (or type of consumer). It is vital that regulators understand this difference to avoid any unintended consequences that would limit the ability of businesses to employ efficient, pro-consumer pricing strategies.

evidence_quotes.quote: Limiting businesses' ability to employ dynamic pricing would almost certainly lead to permanently higher prices for tickets and hotel rooms.

paragraphs.text: Limiting businesses' ability to employ dynamic pricing would almost certainly lead to permanently higher prices for tickets and hotel rooms. Similarly, making it harder for travel businesses to utilise 'starting' prices will make it harder for a business like Skyscanner, that shows offers from over 1,200 suppliers, to help consumers digest information quickly and identify the best offer for them.

evidence_quotes.quote: deceptive or manipulative use of 'starting' prices

paragraphs.text: Once again, we believe it would be better for the Commission to focus on better enforcing against the deceptive or manipulative use of 'starting' prices, which it already has the power to do.

evidence_quotes.quote: "misleading practices regarding price comparisons based on vague reference prices that give a false impression of reduction of the selling price"

paragraphs.text: Regarding price comparisons, again we believe that "misleading practices regarding price comparisons based on vague reference prices that give a false impression of reduction of the selling price" is already clearly against existing law.

evidence_quotes.quote: The proposal to limit the use of percentages or absolute values to communicate price advantages is unclear and raises the risk of restricting the ability for consumers to easily understand and compare different offers.
The wording of the proposal on price comparisons, however, could suggest that a business can only communicate price advantages when comparing one trader with another.

paragraphs.text: The proposal to limit the use of percentages or absolute values to communicate price advantages is unclear and raises the risk of restricting the ability for consumers to easily understand and compare different offers. The wording of the proposal on price comparisons, however, could suggest that a business can only communicate price advantages when comparing one trader with another.

evidence_quotes.quote: discounts can often be based on a reference price from the same trader (for example, if there is a sale or a discount).

Price comparisons are also often widely employed to communicate savings based on average prices for that product, which is another long-established practice that can quickly indicate to consumers when the price they are seeing is cheaper than usual (for a particular flight route at a particular time of year, for example).

paragraphs.text: However, we would note that discounts can often be based on a reference price from the same trader (for example, if there is a sale or a discount). Price comparisons are also often widely employed to communicate savings based on average prices for that product, which is another long-established practice that can quickly indicate to consumers when the price they are seeing is cheaper than usual (for a particular flight route at a particular time of year, for example).

evidence_quotes.quote: communicate price advantages in the ways that are easiest for consumers to understand.

paragraphs.text: This proposal needs to be made much clearer, but we generally find it surprising that the Commission may consider making it harder for businesses to communicate price advantages in the ways that are easiest for consumers to understand. That is not, in our view, in line with a pro-consumer agenda.

evidence_quotes.quote: the fair use of subscriptions.

paragraphs.text: While Skyscanner itself does not offer direct transactions with our users, our model means that we refer traffic to many businesses down-stream of us who do contract with users. One issue that we believe is fundamental to ensuring that consumers are treated fairly when booking travel is the fair use of subscriptions.

evidence_quotes.quote: deceptive user interface design that either obscures the fact that a user is entering into a subscription
making it difficult to cancel such a subscription (such as adding friction into the cancellation process by requiring consumers call customer support)
such as by auto-selecting "discounted prices" without making clear accessing this price is contingent on entering into a subscription

paragraphs.text: We believe that existing law is already clear that deceptive user interface design that either obscures the fact that a user is entering into a subscription (such as by auto-selecting "discounted prices" without making clear accessing this price is contingent on entering into a subscription) or that make it difficult to cancel such a subscription (such as adding friction into the cancellation process by requiring consumers call customer support) are against the law.

evidence_quotes.quote: free trial converts into a paid, annual subscription

paragraphs.text: Similarly, failing to provide sufficiently clear, timely reminders before a free trial converts into a paid, annual subscription is already against the law.

evidence_quotes.quote: There are already clear requirements around online subscriptions in the EU and these should be strongly enforced.

paragraphs.text: There are already clear requirements around online subscriptions in the EU and these should be strongly enforced. For example:

evidence_quotes.quote: whether a contract will renew automatically
renewal terms

paragraphs.text: The CRD requires businesses to give consumers clear, precise, and unambiguous information regarding contract duration, renewal terms, and termination procedures. There are also requirements on informing consumers whether a contract will renew automatically, and giving sufficient information on the terms of that renewal (including costs).

evidence_quotes.quote: free trials that convert into paid subscriptions
cancellation process for any contracts, including subscriptions.

paragraphs.text: For free trials that convert into paid subscriptions, the CRD requires contracts to clearly state that a payment obligation will follow the free trial period unless the consumer cancels before it ends. Businesses must offer a clear and easily accessible cancellation process for any contracts, including subscriptions.
The UCPD mandates that businesses do not impose burdensome or disproportionate non-contractual barriers to termination.

evidence_quotes.quote: shall not design, organise or operate their online interfaces in a way that deceives or manipulates the recipients of their service

paragraphs.text: The DSA states that online platforms "shall not design, organise or operate their online interfaces in a way that deceives or manipulates the recipients of their service or in a way that otherwise materially distorts or impairs the ability of the recipients of their service to make free and informed decisions."

evidence_quotes.quote: the law on subscriptions

paragraphs.text: With the law on subscriptions already being clear, and subscription models becoming more prevalent across multiple sectors, we would welcome greater enforcement of existing law from the Commission.
The growth in overlapping consumer protection obligations has led to a proliferation of consumer notification requirements. While we absolutely support efforts to ensure that

evidence_quotes.quote: duplicative information during the booking process.

paragraphs.text: consumers have access to all the information they need, when they need it, in order to make well-informed decisions, there are cases where consumers are presented with duplicative information during the booking process.

evidence_quotes.quote: consumers are often presented with the same information multiple times when searching for or booking travel via an intermediary, with multiple traders required to surface the same information.

paragraphs.text: This is particularly the case in travel, where consumers are often presented with the same information multiple times when searching for or booking travel via an intermediary, with multiple traders required to surface the same information. This can lead to information overload and click fatigue for consumers.
We therefore welcome the Commission’s commitment to simplification, and to reducing burdens on business without reducing consumer protections.

Given that there appears to be a substantial amount of uncertainty both on the part of some businesses and national enforcement bodies regarding the application of existing consumer law to online practices, we do not believe that it would be helpful for the Commission to alter definitions and concepts that are actually well-established and understood.

evidence_quotes.quote: Altering the established standard of consumer protection

paragraphs.text: Altering the established standard of consumer protection, which is based on the concept of a “reasonably well-informed and reasonably observant and circumspect” consumer (i.e., the average consumer) would undermine legal certainty and understanding when the DFA should be seeking to bolster this.

Similarly, the notion of a “vulnerable” consumer is already well-established in multiple legal frameworks (including the GDPR and DSA). Further expanding the definition to cover vaguer terms such as “emotional distress” or “negative mental state” would undermine clarity and could be hard for businesses to implement in practice.

evidence_quotes.quote: personalised pricing for loyalty programme members
subscriptions

paragraphs.text: Meanwhile, reversing the burden of proof in cases where consumers, interested parties or authorities have disproportionate difficulty in obtaining information to prove a trader’s wrongdoing would risk higher compliance costs for businesses and reduce the number of consumer-friendly practices (such as legitimate user interface designs that point users to vendors with the highest user satisfaction scores, or personalised pricing for loyalty programme members) available to consumers. We believe that many breaches of the law on issues including subscriptions are obvious.

Regarding all the specific policy issues discussed above, we believe that existing EU law is clear and already prohibits the unfair actions identified. Where continued instances of these practices occur, it is not because there are gaps in the legal framework, but rather because of insufficient enforcement of the current framework.

To improve enforcement and compliance, and thus ensure that EU consumers enjoy the high level of protections that they benefit from in theory, we believe that the Digital Fairness Act initiative should focus on improving clarity for businesses and national authorities, and harmonising enforcement approaches. To this end, we would welcome:

○ A regulatory pause. With existing laws already offering consumers a high level of protection, and additional digital laws either recently enacted or still being implemented, it is right that the Commission first focus on better implementing existing law, before allowing for a period of evaluation. Only then, should there be continued issues, would it make sense for the Commission to consider additional rules. Imposing additional rules at this time would only complicate compliance and enforcement further and send a confusing signal to businesses regarding the EU’s commitment to simplification and greater competitiveness.

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns
unfair marketing related to pricing
issues with digital contracts

paragraphs.text: ○ Further clarity on how existing laws – including the UCPD, GDPR, DSA, etc. – apply to dark patterns, unfair marketing related to pricing, and issues with digital contracts, via further guidance (we deal separately with unfair personalisation and social media influencers below).

evidence_quotes.quote: user interface design
personalisation

paragraphs.text: This guidance should provide a non-exhaustive list of practices that are definitely harmful, and a similar one of practices that are acceptable, clearly delineating between instances where particular practices (such as user interface design or personalisation) are harmful and where they are beneficial.

Guidance should include illustrative examples and remain principles-based rather than prescriptive. This would give businesses greater confidence to continue employing pro-consumer approaches, and national enforcement authorities to identify illegal practices online. It should also help to align enforcement approaches across member states, by creating a shared understanding among consumer agencies on how existing law applies to online practices.
The Commission should engage with businesses and other stakeholders on the drafting of the guidance.

evidence_quotes.quote: unfair personalisation practices.

paragraphs.text: ○ An additional, cross-regime compliance guidance document on how the obligations contained in different files – specifically the UCPD, GDPR, DSA, AI Act, and DMA – interact with each other in regard to unfair personalisation practices. The recent guidelines on the interplay between the GDPR and DSA were a welcome start, and this approach should be built upon.

evidence_quotes.quote: Regarding social media influencers, we do believe that there is already extensive existing guidance on the disclosure of advertisements.

paragraphs.text: ○ Regarding social media influencers, we do believe that there is already extensive existing guidance on the disclosure of advertisements. It would be beneficial, however, for this guidance to be updated to clarify how existing obligations should be applied to platform-based advertising features (including, for example,
how the existing rules apply when social media content is displayed on or integrated into platforms).

evidence_quotes.quote: ensure that influencers are complying with all relevant laws

paragraphs.text: The Commission should avoid imposing new obligations on businesses to ensure that influencers are complying with all relevant laws, beyond existing approaches including contracts and training, since this would be disproportionate.
The Commission should boost mechanisms for joint enforcement action, to improve harmonisation. This could include:

evidence_quotes.quote: annual sweeps by the CPC focussed on subscriptions across different sectors, including travel.

paragraphs.text: Increasing the number of regulatory sweeps conducted jointly by the Commission and national enforcement authorities to identify common problems and work with businesses to address issues, based on updated guidance of how existing law applies to online practices. There could, for example, be annual sweeps by the CPC focussed on subscriptions across different sectors, including travel.
Prioritising enforcement action on high-risk sectors and repeat offenders.

evidence_quotes.quote: expandable summaries, icons, pop-up information windows, and progressive disclosure

paragraphs.text: On simplification, we believe that the EU regulatory regime should allow the use of more user-friendly display methods when it comes to important consumer information, such as expandable summaries, icons, pop-up information windows, and progressive disclosure. These approaches ensure that consumers receive the right level of information at the most suitable point of their purchasing journey. Particularly on sites such as Skyscanner, where users are already presented with a significant amount of information (due to the number of offers presented from multiple providers) these approaches avoid overloading consumers with too much information, which makes it harder for them to easily compare and identify offers.

evidence_quotes.quote: duplicative disclosure requirements
display the same information as part of the same booking journey

paragraphs.text: The Commission should also continue to identify duplicative disclosure requirements and seek to remove them where this makes sense. This could include where multiple traders are required to display the same information as part of the same booking journey.

The Commission should not reverse the burden of proof in certain cases. Instead, the Commission could review whether itself and national enforcement authorities have the tools they need to require businesses to share information with them during investigations (or other non-investigatory processes).

As part of broader efforts to improve the clarity of how existing rules apply to online practices, the Commission should avoid changing well-established legal concepts such as the average consumer and vulnerable consumer.
The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.
